reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies episodic reinforcement learning in adversarial linear mixture Markov decision processes with unknown transition kernels and bandit feedback. The authors propose LSUOB-REPS, an occupancy-measure-based algorithm that maintains an ellipsoid confidence set over the linear mixture parameters using a novel “imaginary next-state” regression target. At each episode, LSUOB-REPS computes an upper occupancy bound to construct an optimistically biased loss estimator and updates its policy via online mirror descent over plausible occupancy measures. The paper proves a high-probability regret upper bound of Õ(d S² H S A K) and matches Ω(d H S A K) with a matching lower bound up to logarithmic factors, thus resolving an open problem.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Originality: The work addresses the first provably efficient algorithm for adversarial linear mixture MDPs with unknown transition and bandit feedback, solving the open problem of He et al. (2022). The imaginary next-state regression is novel. Related work is comprehensive and well cited. Quality: The theoretical development is rigorous, with clear decomposition of regret into optimization, bias, and estimation error terms. The occupancy measure difference lemma is a key technical contribution. However, the regret bound suffers an extra S² factor compared to the lower bound, and the algorithm’s computational cost may be high in large MDPs. Clarity: The exposition is thorough but notation-heavy; some proofs are deferred to the appendix. Reproducibility: The algorithm is defined precisely, but no empirical evaluation or code is provided.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Clarity: The paper is well organized, with clear problem setup, algorithm description, and analysis outline. The heavy notation may challenge readers, but key ideas are highlighted. Quality: Technical claims are supported by lemmas and proof sketches, and the matching lower bound strengthens confidence in optimality. Novelty: Introducing the imaginary next-state regression and extending occupancy-measure methods to this setting represent meaningful advances. Reproducibility: The algorithm and parameter settings are specified in detail, enabling theoretical replication. Lack of empirical validation limits assessment of practical performance but does not detract from the theoretical contributions.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This submission makes a significant theoretical advance by proposing the first provably efficient algorithm for adversarial linear mixture MDPs with unknown transitions and bandit feedback, with matching upper and lower bounds. The methods and proofs are rigorous and novel, though the regret bound has suboptimal S² dependence and no experiments are included.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper addresses the problem of episodic adversarial linear mixture MDPs with unknown transitions and bandit feedback—a setting combining linear function approximation, unknown transition models, and adversarial losses. The authors propose LSUOB-REPS, an occupancy-measure-based algorithm that: (1) estimates the transition parameter via least-squares regression on an “imaginary” next-state to form ellipsoidal confidence sets; (2) constructs an optimistically biased loss estimator using an upper occupancy bound and implicit exploration; and (3) updates policies by online mirror descent over occupancy measures consistent with the confidence set. They prove that LSUOB-REPS achieves Õ(d S^2 √(H S A K)) high-probability regret and establish a matching lower bound Ω(d H √(S A K)) up to logarithmic factors, resolving an open problem of He et al. (2022).
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
• Originality: First provably efficient algorithm for adversarial linear mixture MDPs with both unknown transition and bandit feedback. Introduces novel regression on an imaginary next-state and a new occupancy-measure difference lemma. 
• Technical quality: Rigorous regret analysis with upper and matching lower bounds; careful handling of confidence sets and biased estimators. 
• Significance: Advances theoretical understanding at the intersection of adversarial RL and linear function approximation; solves a notable open problem. 
• Related work: Thorough engagement with tabular and linear-approximation adversarial RL literature. 
Weaknesses:
• Dependence on S^2 in regret bound leaves a significant gap versus lower bound; paper acknowledges but leaves open whether S dependence can be reduced. 
• No empirical validation or experiments to illustrate practical performance. 
• Presentation dense: Algorithm and proofs require high expertise; more intuition or examples could aid accessibility.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: The paper is well organized, with definitions, algorithm pseudocode, and proof sketches clearly separated. However, the exposition is highly technical and could benefit from illustrative examples or diagrams to convey core ideas more intuitively. 
Quality: The theoretical contributions are sound, with detailed analysis of each regret component and a novel occupancy measure lemma. All claims are supported by proofs. 
Novelty: The regression-on-imaginary-next-state technique and the extension of occupancy-measure OMD to unknown transitions in adversarial settings are novel and nontrivial. 
Reproducibility: The algorithm is specified in sufficient detail (parameters, update rules, confidence sets). While no code is provided, an expert can reproduce the theoretical results and implement LSUOB-REPS based on the pseudocode and parameter choices.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This manuscript makes a strong theoretical contribution by proposing LSUOB-REPS for adversarial linear mixture MDPs with unknown transitions and bandit feedback, achieving near-optimal regret in d, K, and A and resolving an open problem. The key innovations include least-squares regression on an imaginary next-state and a novel occupancy-measure difference bound. The analysis is rigorous and the related work coverage is comprehensive. The main limitations are the S^2 factor gap to the lower bound and the lack of empirical validation. I recommend acceptance based on the paper’s originality, technical depth, and significance to the field.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper addresses reinforcement learning in episodic adversarial linear mixture MDPs with unknown transition kernels and bandit feedback. The authors propose LSUOB-REPS, an occupancy‐measure‐based algorithm that maintains an ellipsoid confidence set over transition parameters via a novel “imaginary next‐state” regression target and constructs an optimistic loss estimator using upper occupancy bounds with implicit exploration. They prove that LSUOB‐REPS achieves a high‐probability regret bound of Õ(d S^2 H S A K) and establish a matching lower bound of Ω(d H S A K) up to logarithmic factors in d, K, and A. This work resolves an open problem by He et al. (2022), marking the first provably efficient sublinear‐regret algorithm in this challenging setting.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Originality: The imaginary next‐state regression target and the resulting occupancy measure difference lemma are novel contributions that extend occupancy‐measure methods to linear mixture MDPs with bandit feedback and unknown transitions. Related work is comprehensive.
Quality: The theoretical analysis is rigorous and builds on established concentration and online mirror descent techniques. However, the upper bound’s dependence on S^2 and A appears suboptimal compared to lower bounds, leaving a gap. The algorithm’s computational complexity, driven by repeated ellipsoid updates and COMP‐UOB calls, is substantial, and no empirical evaluation is provided to assess practical performance.
Clarity: The paper is densely written. Key definitions, such as the imaginary next‐state selection, require multiple passes to understand. Proofs are deferred to appendices, which hampers accessibility. More intuitive explanations and illustrative examples would improve readability.
Significance: The work solves a known open problem and advances the theoretical frontier in adversarial RL with function approximation. Yet, the heavy dependence on S and A may limit real‐world applicability, and the lack of experiments reduces practical impact.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: The manuscript clearly states its main contributions and places them in context, but the dense notation and deferred proofs make the presentation challenging for non‐experts. Section transitions could be smoother, and pseudocode could be better annotated.
Quality: Contributions are technically sound, with solid regret analyses and lower bounds. The algorithmic framework is appropriate but complex, and computational costs are not fully characterized.
Novelty: Introducing imaginary next‐state regression and an occupancy measure difference lemma is an original approach that fills a gap in the literature on adversarial linear mixture MDPs.
Reproducibility: The pseudocode in Algorithm 1 and references to COMP‐UOB provide a blueprint for implementation. However, the absence of empirical validation, hyperparameter details, and runtime analysis may hinder practitioners from reproducing results or evaluating the algorithm’s practicality.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper presents a technically rigorous algorithm that resolves a major open problem in adversarial linear mixture MDPs with bandit feedback and unknown transitions. While the theoretical contributions are significant and novel, the heavy S^2 A dependence in the regret bound, high computational complexity, and dense presentation without empirical evaluation raise concerns about practical impact and accessibility. I find the work borderline.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies adversarial episodic reinforcement learning in linear mixture MDPs with unknown transition dynamics under bandit feedback. The authors propose LSUOB-REPS, an occupancy‐measure‐based algorithm that maintains a confidence ellipsoid for the transition parameters via a novel least‐squares estimation using an “imaginary” next‐state regression target. They combine this with an implicitly exploratory, optimistically biased loss estimator and online mirror descent over occupancy measures. The main theoretical result is a high‐probability regret upper bound of order Õ(d S² √(H S A K)), together with a matching lower bound Ω(d H √(S A K)) up to logarithmic factors. This resolves an open problem on obtaining sublinear regret in the challenging setting of unknown linear mixture transition and adversarial losses with bandit feedback.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Originality: The work addresses a novel combination of adversarial losses, linear mixture transition models, unknown dynamics and bandit feedback. The imaginary next‐state regression target is a creative extension of value‐targeted estimation. Related work is thoroughly cited. Quality: The paper offers rigorous proofs and a matching lower bound, solving a clear open problem. However, the dependence on S² in the regret may be loose and could limit practical applicability. The analysis is technically dense and omits any empirical validation. Clarity: The manuscript is well structured, but the notation and length of the derivations may hinder accessibility. Some steps are relegated to appendices, making it hard to track key arguments. Significance: By providing the first efficient algorithm in this setting and closing an open question, the paper advances theoretical RL. Yet the lack of experiments or case studies leaves questions on practical impact and parameter tuning.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Clarity: The presentation follows standard structure but uses heavy notation and lengthy technical lemmas, which could be streamlined for readability. Quality: The theoretical contributions are solid, with detailed proof sketches and decomposition of regret terms. Novelty: The imaginary next‐state regression and the resulting occupancy‐measure difference lemma are novel and of independent interest. Reproducibility: The algorithm is fully specified in pseudocode, but no code or empirical section is provided. Key parameters (e.g. learning rate, confidence radius) are given in theory, facilitating future implementation.

Overall, it makes a meaningful contribution to theoretical RL and merits acceptance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper investigates the representational power of two-layer neural networks for approximating solutions of nonlinear variational elliptic PDEs of the form –div(∇L(∇u))=0, whose solutions minimize an energy functional E(u)=∫ΩL(∇u)dx. Building on Barron’s classical bound relating network size to the spectral (Barron) norm of a function, the authors show that if composing L with a function of Barron norm b increases that norm by at most a factor polynomial in dimension, then the PDE solution admits an ε-approximation in L2 by a function with Barron norm O((d B_L)^p log(1/ε)). Consequently, a two-layer network of polynomial size in dimension and log(1/ε) suffices to overcome the curse of dimensionality. The core technique is a “neural unfolding” of preconditioned gradient descent in H1_0(Ω), proving strong convexity of the energy, exponential convergence in the Hilbert norm, and controlled growth of Barron norm per iterate. The results generalize and subsume prior analyses limited to linear elliptic PDEs.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Originality: The paper takes a novel step beyond existing theoretical work on neural PDE solvers by addressing nonlinear variational elliptic equations. Combining Barron-norm analysis with an explicit simulation of gradient flow is new and technically nontrivial. Related work is well cited and distinctions clearly drawn.
Quality: The mathematical arguments are rigorous, leveraging strong convexity in H1_0, spectral Barron bounds, and detailed operator inequalities. Assumptions on L’s composition property are transparent but may be restrictive in practice. The proofs are comprehensive and complete.
Clarity: The paper is well organized, with a clear roadmap, precise definitions, and statements of lemmas and theorems. However, the dense technical sections would benefit from more intuitive exposition and examples illustrating the key steps.
Significance: This work substantially advances our theoretical understanding of neural network solvers for a broad family of nonlinear PDEs, demonstrating curse-of-dimensionality avoidance under natural complexity measures. It lays a solid foundation for future extensions to other nonvariational PDEs and deeper networks.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: The presentation is generally clear, with well-structured theorems and supporting lemmas. Some technical proofs in the appendix are dense; adding a high-level intuition section could enhance readability.  
Quality: Theoretical contributions are sound and thoroughly justified. The assumptions are stated explicitly and the analysis is careful about constant dependencies. 
Novelty: Extending representational complexity bounds from linear to nonlinear variational elliptic PDEs is a significant and original contribution. The combination of Barron norm growth control with preconditioned gradient descent unfolding is innovative.  
Reproducibility: The paper is purely theoretical and provides full proofs. All definitions and assumptions are given explicitly, enabling other researchers to verify and build upon the results.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper presents a rigorous and novel theoretical framework showing that two-layer neural networks can approximate solutions of nonlinear variational elliptic PDEs with polynomial network size, evading the curse of dimensionality under natural Barron-norm assumptions on the energy density L. The contributions extend prior analyses limited to linear PDEs, combining strong convexity in H1_0, preconditioned gradient descent unfolding, and spectral Barron-norm bounds. The work is original, technically sound, and significant to the theory of neural PDE solvers. Minor improvements in exposition and illustrative examples would enhance clarity. Overall, the paper merits acceptance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies the representational power of two-layer neural networks for solving nonlinear variational elliptic PDEs of the form −div(∇L(∇u))=0. Extending prior work limited to linear PDEs, the authors introduce a notion of complexity via the spectral Barron norm and show that if L induces at most polynomial growth in Barron norm (∥L◦f∥_B≤B_L∥f∥_B^p), then the PDE solution u* admits an ε-approximation in L² by a function of Barron norm O((dB_L)^p log(1/ε)). They “neurally unfold” a preconditioned gradient descent in H₀¹(Ω), proving strong convexity of the energy, exponential convergence in O(log(1/ε)) iterations, and bounded Barron‐norm growth per step. Combining with Barron’s classical network‐size bound yields a two‐layer network of size polynomial in dimension and 1/ε, thereby evading the curse of dimensionality. The result subsumes prior linear‐PDE bounds and applies to nonlinear variational problems when L is low‐complexity (e.g. multivariate polynomials).
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Originality: The extension of representational complexity theory to nonlinear variational elliptic PDEs is novel and addresses a key gap beyond linear theory. The Barron‐norm growth framework and neural unfolding technique are creative adaptations of optimization perspectives in function space. Quality: The analysis is rigorous, leveraging strong convexity in H¹ and spectral bounds for the Barron norm. Lemmas are carefully stated, and classical convergence proofs are faithfully adapted. However, dependence on the growth exponent p and constant B_L may hide exponential dimension factors for general L, and further discussion on concrete non‐polynomial L would strengthen applicability. Clarity: The manuscript is densely written, with heavy notation and lengthy proofs. A concise roadmap of assumptions and main proof steps upfront would help readability. Significance: The paper breaks new ground for neural PDE solvers in representational theory, offering potential guidance for designing architectures for nonlinear variational problems. While theoretical, these insights could inspire future algorithmic innovations. Missing are empirical illustrations or case studies on specific nonlinear PDEs to demonstrate that the bounds are tight or predictive.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Clarity: The paper is mathematically precise but could improve presentation by summarizing key definitions and results in a schematic before diving into technical lemmas. Notation overload (e.g. multiple Barron‐norm variants) may challenge non‐specialist readers. Quality: Theorems, lemmas and proofs appear sound and draw appropriately on classical PDE and Barron‐space results. Novelty: This work moves beyond existing linear PDE representational bounds and offers a general representational complexity framework for a large class of nonlinear variational PDEs. Reproducibility: As a purely theoretical contribution, experimental reproduction is not applicable, but all assumptions (domain geometry, L approximation, Barron‐norm algebra) are clearly stated. To facilitate future algorithmic application, one could provide pseudo‐code for the unfolding method and examples of specific L satisfying the growth condition.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This is a technically solid and original theoretical contribution that extends neural network representational bounds from linear to nonlinear variational elliptic PDEs. The Barron‐norm growth condition and neural unfolding of gradient descent yield polynomial network‐size guarantees, subsuming prior linear results. While proofs are rigorous, the manuscript would benefit from clearer organization, illustrative examples of L beyond polynomials, and discussion of the sharpness of bounds. Despite being purely theoretical, the work is significant and opens directions for designing neural solvers for nonlinear PDEs. I recommend acceptance with minor revisions to improve presentation and practical contextualization.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies the representational power of two-layer neural networks (Barron functions) for approximating solutions to nonlinear variational elliptic PDEs of the form −div(∇L(∇u))=0. Under an assumption that composing a function with Barron norm b by L increases its Barron norm by at most B_L b^p, the authors simulate a preconditioned gradient descent in H₀¹(Ω) and bound the exponential convergence and Barron norm growth at each iteration. They prove that the PDE solution can be ε-approximated in L² by a Barron function of norm O((d B_L)^p log(1/ε)), implying a polynomial-sized two-layer network. This generalizes prior results limited to linear elliptic PDEs.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Originality: The extension from linear to nonlinear variational PDEs is novel and addresses a clear gap. Related work is comprehensively cited.  Quality: The theoretical analysis is rigorous, with detailed proofs of strong convexity, convergence rates, and Barron norm recursion. However, key assumptions on L’s complexity growth (B_L,p) may be restrictive or difficult to verify in practice.  Clarity: The manuscript is dense and notation-heavy; core ideas can be obscured by technical details.  Significance: Provides an important theoretical advance, but lacks empirical validation or concrete examples illustrating applicability.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Presentation is mathematically precise, with explicit assumptions and lemmas that enable reproduction of the theoretical bounds. Novelty lies in the representational analysis for nonlinear variational elliptic PDEs, a significant step beyond prior linear results. However, the technical density and absence of illustrative examples or code limit practical reproducibility and accessibility to broader audiences.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper offers a rigorous and novel theoretical framework showing that two-layer networks can avoid the curse of dimensionality when approximating solutions to nonlinear variational elliptic PDEs. Its strength lies in detailed convergence and Barron norm analyses that generalize linear PDE results. Limitations include restrictive assumptions on the nonlinearity, a dense presentation, and no empirical or example-driven validation. With clearer exposition and illustrative case studies, the work would have greater impact.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies the representational power of two-layer neural networks (via Barron norms) to approximate solutions of high-dimensional nonlinear variational elliptic PDEs of the form –div(∇L(∇u))=0, which arise as Euler–Lagrange equations minimizing E(u)=∫ΩL(∇u)dx. Prior theory on neural PDE solvers has focused on linear PDEs; this work extends to nonlinear L by assuming a compositional Barron-norm bound: if composing any Barron function with L increases its norm by at most a factor B_L·b^p, then the PDE solution can be ε-approximated in L2 by a function of Barron norm O((dB_L)^p log(1/ε)). Via Barron’s 1993 result, this yields a two-layer network of size polynomial in dimension that evades the curse of dimensionality. The proof unfolds a preconditioned gradient descent in H^1_0(Ω), showing exponential convergence to u* and bounding Barron-norm growth per iteration using algebraic and Fourier-based lemmas.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Originality: The paper addresses an open theoretical question by extending representational bounds from linear to nonlinear variational PDEs, a novel contribution. Related work is comprehensively cited. Quality: The analysis is rigorous, combining strong convexity in Hilbert space, preconditioned gradient-descent convergence, and careful Barron-norm algebra. Assumptions (e.g., approximability of L by a Barron-friendly ˜L) are clearly stated. However, the practical relevance depends on verifying Assumption 1 for PDEs of interest beyond polynomials, and no experiments validate the bounds. Clarity: The paper is well organized; definitions and technical lemmas are clearly presented, though the burden of notation and dense proofs may challenge some readers. Reproducibility: As a theoretical work, reproducibility concerns the correctness of proofs; detailed appendices aid verification. Novelty & Significance: Advancing curse-of-dimensionality theory to nonlinear PDEs is significant. The representational bounds provide a foundation for future algorithmic developments, though concrete examples or empirical confirmation are lacking.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Clarity: The manuscript is clearly structured with precise definitions, theorems, and proof sketches; dense technical sections may be difficult but are supported by appendices. Quality: The work is technically sound, with rigorous convergence and norm-growth analyses under well-motivated assumptions. Novelty: Extends deep theoretical results on neural PDE approximation from linear to broad classes of nonlinear variational PDEs, a substantive advance. Reproducibility: All theoretical arguments are detailed with proofs in the appendix; assumptions and constants are explicit, allowing independent validation. No code is needed.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper rigorously demonstrates that neural networks can evade the curse of dimensionality for a class of nonlinear variational elliptic PDEs by bounding the Barron norm of solutions and thus network size. The theoretical contribution is original, well supported, and clearly presented, though its practical impact would benefit from concrete examples or experiments. Overall, this advance in neural PDE theory merits acceptance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper introduces TOIS, the first single-stage transformer-based framework for open-world instance segmentation (OWIS). Building on Mask2Former, TOIS adds a lightweight foreground prediction branch and defines a novel cross-task consistency loss between the foreground map and the union of instance masks. This loss down-weights the penalty for unlabeled or incompletely annotated objects and enables effective handling of incomplete annotations. The authors further extend TOIS to a semi-supervised setting by applying the consistency loss to unlabeled images, demonstrating that models trained with as little as 30% labeled data outperform fully supervised counterparts with 50% annotations. Extensive experiments on UVO, COCO→UVO, Cityscapes→Mapillary, and ablation studies validate significant improvements over state-of-the-art OWIS and semi-supervised baselines.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Originality: The paper addresses OWIS with a novel single-stage transformer architecture and a cross-task consistency regularization, distinguishing it from prior two-stage or affinity-based methods. The semi-supervised extension is a compelling contribution. Quality: The method is technically sound, with clear derivation of losses and a complete end-to-end implementation. Experimental comparisons are comprehensive, covering intra- and cross-dataset settings and ablations on pseudo-labeling and consistency loss. Clarity: The manuscript is generally well written, with clear diagrams and thorough experimental details. Some sections could be streamlined to improve readability, especially the pseudo-labeling description. Significance: Substantial AP and AR gains (4–12%) over state of the art and strong semi-supervised performance suggest high impact. The approach is likely to influence future OWIS and segmentation research.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Clarity: The paper is organized logically, with clear problem motivation and method description. Figures effectively illustrate key ideas. A few implementation details (e.g., hyperparameter tuning for loss weights) could be elaborated. Quality: The design of the consistency loss and its integration into the transformer framework are appropriate and well justified. Novelty: Introducing a cross-task consistency loss for OWIS and leveraging it for semi-supervised learning represents a meaningful advancement over existing work. Reproducibility: The use of Detectron2, public backbones, and detailed training protocols will facilitate replication. The authors’ commitment to release code further supports reproducibility.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper presents a novel transformer-based OWIS framework (TOIS) with cross-task consistency loss and a semi-supervised extension, demonstrating strong empirical gains and comprehensive evaluations. The approach is original, technically sound, and clearly presented, with high relevance to the segmentation community. I recommend acceptance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper presents TOIS, the first single-stage transformer-based framework for Open-World Instance Segmentation (OWIS). Building on Mask2Former, the model adds a lightweight foreground prediction branch and a novel cross-task consistency loss that aligns the union of instance masks with a foreground map. This regularization mitigates incomplete annotations common in OWIS datasets and naturally extends to a semi-supervised setting by applying the consistency loss on unlabeled images. Experiments on UVO, COCO→UVO and Cityscapes→Mapillary demonstrate 4–5% AP100 improvements over state-of-the-art, and the semi‐supervised variant trained with 30% labels outperforms a fully‐supervised model trained with 50% labels.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Originality: The integration of a cross-task consistency loss into a transformer segmentation model for OWIS is novel. Framing unlabeled images as extreme annotation gaps is insightful. However, the method is a straightforward extension of Mask2Former with an auxiliary branch and loss.
Quality: Empirical results are strong, with thorough intra- and cross-dataset benchmarks and ablations. Claims are well supported, though theoretical analysis of the consistency loss is absent. Hyperparameter choices (loss weights, thresholds) lack sensitivity analysis.
Clarity: The paper is well organized and clearly written. Figures and tables effectively illustrate results. Some notation in the consistency loss section and ablation tables could be clarified. The semi-supervised protocol is described adequately.
Significance: Open-world segmentation is an important emerging task. The 4–5% AP gains and semi-supervised benefits are substantial, and the method’s simplicity favors adoption. Broader validation on additional real-world scenarios would strengthen impact.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The paper is clearly presented, with a logical flow from problem definition to method and experiments. Contributions are well motivated and compared to prior work. Novelty stems from the cross-task consistency loss applied to OWIS, advancing the field by addressing annotation gaps without complex auxiliary networks. Experimental protocols are detailed (datasets, backbones, GPUs, learning rates), but reproducibility would improve with release of code, explicit training schedules, seed settings and ablation of loss‐weight sensitivity. Overall, clarity and quality are high and the results are novel and largely reproducible.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: TOIS introduces a simple yet effective consistency regularizer that leverages an auxiliary foreground branch to address missing annotations in OWIS and enables semi-supervised learning. The method achieves substantial gains over Mask2Former and other baselines across multiple datasets. The paper is well written and experiments comprehensive. It would benefit from sensitivity analyses of hyperparameters, more theoretical insight on the consistency loss, and evaluation on additional open-world benchmarks. I recommend acceptance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper introduces TOIS, a single-stage transformer-based framework for Open-World Instance Segmentation (OWIS). Building on Mask2Former, the authors add a lightweight foreground prediction branch and define a novel cross-task consistency loss that enforces agreement between the union of instance mask predictions and the predicted foreground map. This regularization mitigates incomplete annotations common in OWIS datasets and extends naturally to semi-supervised learning by applying the consistency loss on unlabeled images. Experiments on UVO, COCO→UVO, and Cityscapes→Mapillary benchmarks demonstrate significant gains over state-of-the-art two-stage and one-stage OWIS methods, improving AP100 by up to 4.75% and AR100 by over 3%. In semi-supervised settings, TOIS trained with 30% labeled data outperforms a fully-supervised model trained with 50% labels. The paper includes extensive ablations on cross-task consistency, pseudo-labeling, and backbone variations.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Strengths: 1) Novel cross-task consistency loss directly targets incomplete annotation in open-world segmentation and yields measurable improvements. 2) Unified single-stage transformer framework simplifies training and inference compared to two-stage approaches. 3) Comprehensive experiments across multiple datasets and settings, including semi-supervised learning, support the claims. 4) Ablation studies isolate the impact of each component. Weaknesses: 1) Contribution is an incremental modification of Mask2Former; the core transformer architecture remains unchanged. 2) The choice and weighting of loss terms (α, β, γ, ω) lack sensitivity analysis; hyperparameter tuning details are sparse. 3) Limited discussion of computational overhead and inference speed trade-offs introduced by the foreground branch during training. 4) Some comparisons omit recent open-world baselines (e.g., DETR-based adaptations). 5) Notation in Section 3 is dense and occasionally ambiguous, hindering reproducibility.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Clarity: The paper is generally well structured, with clear motivation and thorough literature review. However, formulas in Section 3.3 and the notation for pseudo-label generation would benefit from a concise table of symbols. Quality: The proposed consistency loss is well motivated and empirically justified. Experimental methodology is sound, though more analysis of variance and training stability would strengthen the results. Novelty: Introducing a cross-task consistency constraint in OWIS is a fresh idea; adapting transformer instance segmentation to the open-world and semi-supervised domains addresses a practical gap. Reproducibility: The authors plan to release code, but key implementation details—such as loss weight selection, convergence criteria, and data preprocessing—are only briefly described. Including a pseudo-code algorithm or hyperparameter ranges would improve reproducibility.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper presents a compelling extension of transformer instance segmentation to open-world and semi-supervised settings via a cross-task consistency loss. The method achieves strong empirical gains, but the core architecture remains an incremental Mask2Former adaptation. Key weaknesses include limited hyperparameter analysis, incomplete baseline comparisons, and minor clarity issues in the loss formulation. Overall, the work advances OWIS but requires additional clarity and broader benchmarking to fully assess its impact.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper introduces TOIS, a single-stage transformer-based framework for open-world instance segmentation (OWIS). Unlike prior two-stage OWIS methods that generate bounding boxes then masks, TOIS concurrently predicts class-agnostic instance masks and a global foreground map via an added convolutional branch. A novel cross-task consistency loss penalizes disagreement between the union of predicted instance masks and the foreground map, mitigating the impact of incomplete annotations common in OWIS datasets. The authors integrate this mechanism into both fully-supervised and semi-supervised training regimes, demonstrating that leveraging unlabeled images with consistency loss yields substantial gains. Extensive experiments on UVO, COCO→UVO, Cityscapes→Mapillary, and ablation studies validate that TOIS outperforms state-of-the-art methods by a wide margin, and that semi-supervised TOIS trained with 30% labeled data can exceed a fully-supervised counterpart using 50% labels.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Originality: The paper adapts transformer-based segmentation to the open-world setting and introduces a cross-task consistency regularizer. While the idea of multi-task consistency is not entirely new, its application to OWIS in a single-stage framework is novel. Related work is comprehensively cited. Quality: The method is technically sound, with clear formulation of losses and a comprehensive training pipeline. Empirical results are thorough, covering intra- and cross-dataset scenarios, backbone variants, pseudo-labeling synergy, and semi-supervised learning. However, theoretical justification for the loss and sensitivity to hyperparameters (loss weights, query count) is not fully analyzed. Clarity: The paper is generally well organized, with clear figures and detailed methodology. Some notation (e.g. matching process, hyperparameter settings) could be better summarized in the main text rather than relegated to the appendix. Significance: The consistent improvements (>4% AP gains) across multiple benchmarks and the promising semi-supervised results suggest practical impact. OWIS is a challenging, underexplored problem, and TOIS advances the state of the art in a demonstrable way.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Clarity of presentation is high: the paper systematically explains motivation, architecture, losses, and training procedures. Figures and tables support the narrative. Quality of contributions is solid, with extensive experiments validating each component. Novelty stems from integrating a foreground branch and cross-task consistency loss into a transformer framework for OWIS; this is a meaningful advance over two-stage and closed-world adaptations. Reproducibility is aided by detailed implementation notes (backbones, learning rates, data augmentation). Pending public code release and more explicit hyperparameter listings would further strengthen reproducibility.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This submission proposes TOIS, a compelling single-stage transformer for open-world instance segmentation with a novel cross-task consistency loss. It convincingly addresses incomplete annotations and extends naturally to semi-supervised learning. The empirical evaluation is thorough and demonstrates clear gains across benchmarks. Minor concerns include limited theoretical analysis of the consistency loss, dependence on hyperparameters, and the incremental nature of the regularization idea. Overall, the paper is a high-quality contribution with both practical significance and solid experimental support.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies nonparametric regression with deep ReLU networks under standard ℓ2 weight decay. Focusing on a parallel‐network architecture, the authors show ℓ2 regularization on network weights is equivalent to an ℓp (p=2/L) sparsity penalty on learned basis coefficients. They prove such networks, with only the regularization parameter tuned, achieve estimation error nearly matching minimax rates over Besov and BV function classes, and that deeper networks exponentially approach the optimal rate. Key technical contributions include an ℓp‐sparse representation equivalence, novel metric‐entropy bounds for overparameterized parallel networks, and matching lower and upper bounds on approximation and estimation errors. Empirical results in 1D illustrate the method’s local adaptivity.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Originality: The work provides a new understanding of ℓ2 regularization in deep networks as inducing ℓp‐sparsity, and shows local adaptivity without architecture tuning, advancing beyond RKHS‐based analyses and prior two‐layer or sparsity‐enforced constructions. Quality: The theoretical development is rigorous, with careful decomposition of approximation and estimation errors, tight covering‐number bounds, and clear minimax‐rate proofs. The assumptions (parallel architecture, global optimum) are standard in recent convex‐duality analyses but merit discussion. Experiments validate adaptivity but are limited to 1D. Clarity: The paper is dense but well‐organized; some proofs and constants are deferred to appendices, which may slow readers. Significance: Establishing that standard ℓ2 weight decay alone yields near‐optimal local adaptivity and explains the depth benefit is an important advance likely to influence theoretical and practical views on regularization in deep learning.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The presentation is generally clear, with a logical flow from problem setup to main theorems and proof sketches. The equivalence to ℓp‐sparse regression is intuitive and well motivated. Technical sections are detailed, though constants and some technical lemmas appear in supplemental material. Novelty is high: no prior work showed ℓ2‐only regularized deep networks adapt across Besov and BV classes. Reproducibility is supported by a thorough experimental description, though code release is not mentioned. Providing reference implementations of the parallel architecture and hyperparameter settings would further aid reproduction.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper gives a novel theoretical framework showing that depth with standard ℓ2 regularization in parallel ReLU networks induces ℓp sparsity and achieves near‐minimax adaptive rates over Besov and BV spaces. The arguments are rigorous and experiments support the theory. I recommend accepting this strong contribution.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper investigates the ability of deep ReLU networks with standard ℓ₂ weight decay to adaptively estimate functions with spatially heterogeneous smoothness, characterized by Besov and bounded variation classes. Focusing on a “parallel” architecture that ensembles multiple subnetworks, the authors prove that weight decay induces an ℓₚ sparsity penalty (p=2/L) on learned basis coefficients and, by tuning only the regularization strength, achieves estimation rates arbitrarily close to the minimax bounds for these function classes. They further show that deeper networks exponentially reduce the gap to optimality and provide matching upper bounds on the model’s metric entropy. Numerical experiments in one dimension illustrate local adaptivity comparable to classical methods (trend filtering, wavelets) and superior scaling in error with sample size.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: The paper establishes a novel equivalence between ℓ₂ regularization in parallel deep ReLU networks and an ℓₚ sparsity penalty, providing foundational insight into why depth enhances adaptivity. Theoretical contributions include near‐minimax risk bounds for Besov and BV classes that require tuning only weight decay, and metric entropy bounds independent of overparameterization. The work unifies spline‐based synthesis and kernel‐based analysis approaches and separates neural networks from linear methods. Experiments confirm theoretical predictions in prototypical one‐dimensional settings.

Weaknesses: The analysis relies on a specialized parallel architecture and global optimum assumptions that may not fully reflect practice. Extensions to multivariate and high‐dimensional regimes are not explored, and the reliance on overparameterization and deep widths raises computational and optimization concerns. Empirical evaluation is limited to one‐dimensional benchmarks, and practical training dynamics under SGD are not rigorously addressed. Some proofs are quite involved, which may challenge readers seeking intuition.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Clarity: The paper is well organized, clearly motivating local adaptivity in nonparametric regression and systematically developing its theoretical framework. Definitions and theorems are stated precisely, though some technical proofs are dense and deferred to appendices. Quality: The theoretical results are sound, building on classical spline and Besov theory and leveraging novel entropy arguments. Novelty: The equivalence between weight decay and ℓₚ sparsity in deep parallel networks is a significant conceptual advance, and the adaptive minimax rates with standard regularization are new. Reproducibility: The authors supply detailed algorithmic descriptions and hyperparameter settings for their one‐dimensional experiments. However, code release is not mentioned, and the global‐optimum assumption may limit direct empirical replication under SGD.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This manuscript delivers a substantial theoretical advance by showing that deep parallel ReLU networks with standard ℓ₂ weight decay automatically adapt to heterogeneous smoothness and achieve near‐minimax rates in Besov and BV function classes. The authors prove an ℓₚ sparsity equivalence, derive metric entropy bounds, and link depth to exponential improvements. While the global‐optimum and specialized architecture assumptions temper generality, the core insights into depth and adaptivity are compelling. Limited high‐dimensional experiments and practical SGD analysis suggest directions for future work. Overall, the contribution is strong, well supported, and merits acceptance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper investigates the ability of deep ReLU neural networks with standard ℓ2 (weight‐decay) regularization to adaptively estimate functions with spatially varying smoothness in classical nonparametric regression settings. Focusing on a “parallel” architecture that sums the outputs of multiple subnetworks, the authors show that training with ℓ2 regularization is equivalent to imposing an ℓp penalty (p=2/L) on learned basis coefficients. Leveraging this equivalence, they prove that by tuning only the regularization parameter, parallel ReLU networks achieve estimation error rates arbitrarily close to the minimax rates for Besov and bounded‐variation (BV) classes, with the gap shrinking exponentially in depth. They also derive covering‐number bounds independent of network width, provide a bias–variance decomposition handling an unconstrained subspace, and present numerical experiments in one dimension comparing against trend filtering, smoothing splines, and wavelet denoising.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Strengths: (1) Novel theoretical insight showing ℓ2‐regularized parallel DNNs induce ℓp‐sparsity, unifying neural networks with classical adaptive regression methods. (2) Comprehensive error analysis yielding near‐minimax rates for both Besov and BV spaces, highlighting the role of depth in closing the optimality gap. (3) Metric‐entropy bounds that do not grow with model width, supporting strong generalization even in highly overparameterized regimes. (4) Empirical demonstrations on toy 1D functions that align with theoretical predictions and outperform kernel and spline methods. Weaknesses: (1) Relies on strong assumptions (availability of global minimizer, exact parallel architecture) that may not hold under SGD dynamics. (2) Constants hidden in ˜O may be large, limiting practical guidance on network size and depth. (3) Experiments restricted to one dimension and synthetic targets; lacks evidence in higher dimensions or real data. (4) Presentation is extremely notation‐heavy and may be inaccessible to practitioners.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Clarity: The paper is densely written with extensive theoretical detail but could benefit from more intuitive explanations and streamlined notation. Quality: The proofs appear technically sound and build on a solid statistical learning framework, though they hinge on global optimization which is not guaranteed in practice. Novelty: The equivalence between weight decay in deep parallel networks and nonconvex ℓp sparsity penalties is a significant advance, explaining depth‐dependent adaptivity in nonparametric regression. Reproducibility: While theoretical arguments are thorough, implementation details (e.g., hyperparameter selection, network initialization, training dynamics) are sparse and could hinder replication; providing code or pseudo‐code would improve accessibility.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper investigates the ability of deep ReLU networks with standard ℓ2 weight decay to adaptively estimate functions in classical nonparametric regression settings, specifically Besov and bounded variation (BV) classes. The authors focus on a "parallel" architecture that ensembles multiple subnetworks. They prove that ℓ2 regularization on an L-layer parallel network is equivalent to enforcing an ℓp sparsity penalty (p=2/L) on learned basis coefficients. Leveraging this equivalence, they derive upper bounds on the mean squared error of the regularized estimator, showing it can approach the minimax rate for Besov and BV classes up to vanishing factors as depth increases. They further develop covering-number bounds independent of width, and demonstrate through simulations that parallel networks exhibit local adaptivity comparable to trend filtering and wavelet methods.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Strengths: The work addresses a fundamental theoretical question—local adaptivity of deep nets—bridging modern deep learning with classical nonparametric theory. The ℓ2→ℓp equivalence is elegant and clarifies the role of depth in promoting sparsity. The covering-number analysis that decouples width from complexity is technically novel. The results unify and extend prior sparse‐NN and RKHS​‐based analyses. Simulations illustrate the method’s adaptivity in 1D settings. Weaknesses: The architecture is specialized (parallel block diagonal), limiting direct applicability to common monolithic networks. The analysis assumes access to the global minimizer and fixed design; practical optimization and random design remain less clear. Results focus on 1D and Besov/BV function classes; extensions to higher-dimensional or other function spaces are not addressed. Empirical evaluation is limited to small synthetic examples.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Clarity: The paper is densely written but well organized, with clear statements of assumptions and theorems. However, some technical proofs are highly compressed, which may hinder accessibility. Quality: The theoretical contributions are rigorous and build upon established techniques, with careful derivations of estimation and approximation errors. Novelty: The insight that standard ℓ2 decay in deep parallel nets induces ℓp sparsity and near-minimax rates is a new and nontrivial advance. Reproducibility: The synthetic experiments are straightforward to replicate; code for trend filtering and network training is standard. Full reproduction would benefit from public release of training scripts and hyperparameter settings.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper makes a significant theoretical contribution by showing that weight-decayed deep ReLU parallel networks can achieve near-minimax local adaptivity in Besov and BV classes through an ℓ2→ℓp sparsity equivalence and depth-driven convergence. While the specialization to parallel architectures and fixed-design context are limitations, the results clarify why depth matters and extend classical nonparametric theory to modern deep nets. The writing is dense but organized, and experiments support the main claims.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes Dateformer, a novel Transformer-based architecture for long‐term time series forecasting that explicitly captures both global and local patterns. It first splits fine‐grained series into day‐level patches to reduce token length and improve information density. A Date Encoder Representations from Transformers (DERT) module learns dynamic date embeddings by pre‐training on mean and autocorrelation prediction tasks, distilling the entire training set’s global information into compact time representations. During inference, Dateformer generates a day‐level global forecast by feeding date representations through a feed‐forward network and a local forecast by learning and aggregating residual patterns from a lookback window via a vanilla Transformer. The final prediction is the sum of global and local components. Experiments on seven real‐world datasets demonstrate up to 33.6% relative MSE improvement over state‐of-the-art baselines and extend forecasting horizons to half‐a-year.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Originality: The combination of patch‐wise day‐level processing, global distillation into time representations, and residual aggregation is novel in the time series domain. Related work is well cited, though the rich static date embedding could be more clearly compared to Learnable Positional Encodings.
Quality: The method is technically sound and accompanied by extensive experiments across diverse datasets, ablations, and analyses. Claims are supported by empirical evidence, though the multi‐stage training (pre‐train, warm‐up, fine‐tune) and date‐feature engineering introduce complexity. Scalability to extremely coarse series or irregular calendars is not shown.
Clarity: The paper is generally well organized, with detailed appendices. However, the notation is dense, and key ideas (e.g., DERT pre‐training, feed‐forward global component) could be more succinctly summarized. The heavy reliance on calendar feature engineering may hinder accessibility.
Significance: Improving long‐range forecasting is important for many applications. The substantial empirical gains and extensible framework suggest that Dateformer will influence future forecasting work and encourage further study of time‐modeling strategies.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The presentation is thorough but very detailed; a more concise schematic of the three stages would aid understanding. The contributions are high‐quality and novel, advancing the field by shifting from point‐wise to patch‐wise and time‐representation learning. Reproducibility is supported by extensive appendices detailing hyperparameters, date embeddings, and ablations, and code release is promised.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Dateformer is a strong, innovative approach that leverages day‐level patches and dynamic date embeddings to capture global and local patterns for long‐term forecasting. The extensive experiments validate its performance, though the method requires multi‐stage training and substantial feature engineering. Overall, the paper makes a meaningful contribution and merits acceptance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper presents Dateformer, a novel Transformer‐based architecture for long‐term time series forecasting. The key ideas are: (1) patch‐wise encoding of raw time series at a daily granularity to reduce token length and increase information density; (2) distilling global historical patterns into ‘‘date representations’’ via a lightweight Transformer encoder (DERT) and two supervised pretraining tasks (mean value and autocorrelation prediction); (3) using date representations to generate a global prediction for each day via a shared feed‐forward network; (4) computing residuals between historical series and their global predictions, then using a cross‐attention Transformer to learn weights that aggregate these residuals into a day‐level local prediction; and (5) fusing global and local predictions and extending via day‐wise autoregression. Experiments on seven diverse datasets (energy, traffic, economics, weather, exchange rates) show 33.6% average relative MSE improvement over state‐of‐the‐art baselines across horizons up to 180 days. Ablations validate the patch‐wise and time representation components, and analyses decompose global/local contributions.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Originality: The shift from point‐wise to day‐level patch processing and the distillation of global series information into date representations constitute a novel combination of ideas in time series forecasting. The DERT pretraining on date semantics is also innovative. Related work is extensively cited.
Quality: The methodology is well supported by comprehensive experiments, including multivariate and univariate settings, ablations on lookback length, patch size, pretraining tasks, and hyperparameter sensitivity. Results are convincing and robust, with standard deviations reported.
Clarity: The presentation is thorough but dense; critical details are deferred to lengthy appendices, which may overwhelm the reader. The static date embedding is feature‐engineered with many calendar variables, requiring careful reading to reproduce.
Significance: Extending forecast horizons to six months with strong accuracy gains is an important advance. The approach is likely to inspire follow‐up work on global context modeling in forecasting.
Weaknesses: The handcrafted date embeddings and multi‐stage training introduce engineering complexity. Scalability beyond daily patterns (e.g., irregularly sampled series) is unclear. Reproducibility hinges on code release and careful hyperparameter tuning.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: The paper is logically structured and motivates each component, but the dense exposition and heavy reliance on appendices hamper quick comprehension. Figures illustrate concepts well, but readers must navigate many sections for implementation details.
Quality: The experimental design is comprehensive, with comparisons to strong baselines, robustness checks, and ablations that validate each novel element.
Novelty: Reformulating time series as day‐level patches and distilling global patterns into learned time representations are fresh contributions that advance forecasting methodology.
Reproducibility: The promised code release is essential. While hyperparameters and architectures are described, the extensive engineered date features and three‐stage training protocol pose challenges. A clear open‐source implementation and detailed training scripts will be necessary for adoption.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This work proposes Dateformer, a Transformer variant that operates on daily patches and distills global historical patterns into learned date representations to improve long‐term forecasting. It achieves significant gains on seven real‐world datasets and is supported by thorough ablations. The contributions are novel and impactful, though the presentation is dense and the engineering complexity—especially in feature engineering and multi‐stage training—may hinder reproducibility. Overall, it offers a compelling advance in time series forecasting.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper presents Dateformer, a time-series forecasting framework that extends the look-back horizon by (1) splitting high-frequency series into daily “patches,” thus reducing token count, and (2) distilling global information from the entire training set into date embeddings via a pre-trained Date Encoder Representations from Transformers (DERT). During inference, Dateformer generates a global forecast from these time representations and a local forecast by modeling residuals over a short look-back window, then sums the two. Experiments on seven real-world multivariate and three univariate datasets demonstrate up to 33.6% average MSE improvement over Transformer-based baselines and extend the feasible forecast range to 180 days on fine-grained series.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Originality: The combination of patch-wise input and distilled time representations is novel in forecasting, though the patching idea parallels ViT in vision. Related work is extensive but some key classical methods (e.g. Prophet, seasonal ARIMA) are omitted. Quality: The method is technically sound and extensively evaluated; however, the added complexity of DERT pre-training per dataset, arbitrary hyperparameter choices (padding sizes, patch size), and heavy autoregressive recursion raise practical concerns. Scalability claims are qualified by 24 GB memory limits and still require day-level aggregation. Clarity: The 11-page main text plus long appendices contain numerous grammatical errors, inconsistent notation (e.g., “throughout” vs. “throughput”), improperly formatted equations and strange character artifacts, making reproduction difficult. Significance: Empirical gains on multiple domains are substantial, but generality beyond daily periodic series and dependence on extensive pre-training are unclear.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Clarity: Presentation is overly dense; long appendices mix essential and peripheral material; inconsistent notation and typos impede comprehension. Quality: The dual-stage global/local forecast design is well motivated, but the heavy model components (DERT, separate distillation, autoregressive residual transformer) add implementation complexity. Novelty: Distilling global series information into time embeddings is a creative advance, though patching resembles established vision techniques. Reproducibility: Many architectural and training hyperparameters (patch size, padding, learning-rate schedule, pre-training tasks) are set without principled guidance; code is promised but unavailable, hindering immediate replication.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Dateformer introduces an innovative time-modeling paradigm that leverages daily patching and distilled temporal embeddings to extend forecast horizons with strong empirical gains. The methodology is sound and results are compelling, but the paper suffers from excessive complexity, unclear hyperparameter selection, substantial per-dataset pre-training overhead, and poor presentation. These factors raise concerns about practical adoption and reproducibility, limiting its broader impact despite promising performance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper addresses the limitation of existing Transformer-based time series models that only attend to a short look-back window and discard the remainder of the training data at inference. The authors propose Dateformer, a pure Transformer framework that operates in two stages: (1) patch-wise splitting by day, converting fine-grained point-wise inputs into daily patches to reduce token count and increase information density; (2) distilling global patterns from the entire training set into per-day time representations (via a date encoder, DERT) and then using these as primary modeling entities. During inference, Dateformer produces a global forecast from the time representations and a local residual forecast from a vanilla Transformer on series residuals, combining them for the final prediction. Experiments on seven real-world datasets demonstrate up to 33.6% average MSE improvement over six strong baselines, with extended forecast horizons up to six months.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Originality: The idea of patch-wise processing for time series adapts the ViT paradigm and is a novel combination with Transformer forecasting. Distilling global information into learned time embeddings is an interesting innovation. However, the high-level concepts echo common decompositions and global/local splits in prior work. Quality: The paper presents extensive experiments across diverse datasets and forecasts, with ablations on lookback length, time encoding, pre-training tasks, and patch sizes. Results consistently favor Dateformer, though it relies on many hyperparameters and dataset-specific DERT pre-training, raising concerns about overfitting and engineering complexity. Clarity: The manuscript is very long and dense; core ideas are buried within extensive appendices. Figures and notation are at times confusing, and key architectural diagrams and algorithmic pseudocode are lacking. Significance: Improving long-range forecasting is important, and the state-of-the-art gains are substantial. The approach could influence future time series modeling, but the heavy pre-training and complexity may limit adoption.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Clarity: Presentation suffers from excessive length and scattered explanations. A more concise description of the core pipeline, diagrams of data flow, and pseudocode would help. Quality: Methodology is technically sound and well supported by experiments, but reliance on many design choices and dataset-specific pre-training reduces generality. Novelty: The patching strategy and global distillation into time embeddings are novel adaptations but build on known ideas in vision Transformers and forecasting decomposition. Reproducibility: The authors promise code release, but reproducing results will require careful re-implementation of DERT pre-training, date embedding algorithms, and multi-stage training schedules. Detailed hyperparameter settings are in the appendix but overall engineering effort is high.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Dateformer presents a creative pure-Transformer architecture for long-term forecasting by patching time series into daily units and distilling global patterns into learned time embeddings. Extensive experiments show strong improvements over competitive baselines. Despite the promising results and interesting ideas, the manuscript is overly long and complex, with many dataset-specific design choices and heavy pre-training requirements. Clarity and reproducibility could be improved by focusing on core components and providing clearer diagrams and code. I recommend a marginal acceptance to encourage refinement.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper introduces AdaStride, a novel downsampling layer for sequential data that learns to apply variable stride lengths across an input sequence given a fixed overall downsampling ratio. The key idea is a vector positioning (VP) process that computes a normalized position for each time step, then constructs an alignment matrix to perform soft downsampling via matrix multiplication (AdaStride) or a more efficient scatter_add variant (AdaStride-F). The method preserves more information in task-relevant regions by allocating smaller strides there and larger strides elsewhere. The authors evaluate AdaStride against uniform pooling, strided convolution, spectral pooling, and DiffStride on three tasks: audio classification, automatic speech recognition (ASR), and discrete representation learning with VQ-VAE. AdaStride consistently outperforms alternatives on most datasets, and extensive analyses illustrate how adaptive strides correlate with information density. Detailed appendices cover algorithmic pseudo-code, hyperparameters, ablations on position loss stability, robustness to input shifts, and computational cost measurements.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Originality: The concept of learning variable downsampling ratios within a sample via a position-based alignment matrix is novel in sequential DL architectures. It extends learnable stride approaches beyond uniform or global parameterization. Related work on DiffStride and spectral pooling is well cited, though references to recent dynamic sampling in other modalities could be added. Quality: The methodology is clearly defined and technically sound, with thorough derivations and stability measures (position loss). Experiments span multiple tasks and datasets, demonstrating consistent gains. The necessity of tuning the sharpness hyperparameter σ and the O(n²) alignment costs are acknowledged but only partially addressed by AdaStride-F and a single AdaStride-S variant. Clarity: The paper is densely written; the core VP idea and alignment construction are described clearly, but heavy mathematical notation and extensive appendices may overwhelm readers. Visualizations of learned alignments aid understanding. Significance: Downsampling is ubiquitous in sequence modeling; applying adaptive strides can broadly benefit speech, audio, and potentially other modalities. The empirical gains are modest but systematic. The high computational overhead and need for dataset-specific σ tuning may limit immediate adoption. Overall, the work represents a genuine advance in adaptive downsampling.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The paper presents a clear narrative: the motivation for adaptive strides, the VP algorithm, and two implementation variants. Core equations are well explained, and extensive appendices supply pseudo-code, hyperparameter tables, and architectural diagrams. Novelty stems from per-step stride adaptation rather than global learnable factors. Experimental quality is high, with comparisons across baselines, ablations, and robustness tests. Reproducibility is supported by supplementary code, detailed training settings, and dataset licenses; however, key hyperparameters (σ variations) are scattered, and computational trade-offs require careful study. Overall, clarity and documentation are above average for complex methods, the contribution is meaningfully novel, and results appear reproducible given the provided artifacts.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: AdaStride offers an innovative approach to downsampling by learning per-step strides via a vector positioning alignment matrix. The method outperforms standard and recent learnable downsampling layers across audio classification, ASR, and discrete representation tasks. Strengths include methodological novelty, comprehensive experiments, and insightful analyses of adaptive stride patterns. Weaknesses involve quadratic computational cost, reliance on a dataset-specific σ hyperparameter, and limited demonstrations beyond audio. The paper is well documented and largely reproducible. Given its potential to improve sequence modeling and extensibility to other domains, I recommend acceptance with minor revisions to clarify hyperparameter settings and computational costs.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposes AdaStride, a novel learnable downsampling layer for sequential data that applies variable strides across a sequence under an overall compression budget. The method constructs an alignment matrix via a “vector positioning” mechanism: each input time step is assigned a continuous position on an output timeline using learned displacements followed by soft normalization. The alignment weights are used to perform weighted pooling, yielding an adaptive downsampling. A variant, AdaStride-F, replaces full matrix multiplication with a sparse scatter_add implementation to reduce cost. The authors evaluate AdaStride on three audio tasks—classification, speech recognition, and VQ-VAE discrete representation learning—showing improvements over standard pooling, strided convolution, spectral pooling, and DiffStride. Experiments analyze learned alignments, hyperparameter σ controlling sharpness, and trade-offs between aliasing and resolution. Ablations include position loss for stability and limited image experiments.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses: Originality: The adaptive stride concept extends learnable downsampling beyond fixed-rate approaches and is novel. Related work is cited but connections to dynamic temporal pooling and attention-based compression could be stronger.Quality: Experiments cover diverse audio tasks and demonstrate consistent gains, but the quadratic complexity of AdaStride, reliance on extensive hyperparameter tuning (σ), and only partial speedup in AdaStride-F raise concerns. The stability term (position loss) is justified empirically but lacks theoretical grounding. The image extension is superficial and shows limited benefit. Clarity: The core idea is well presented, but the paper and appendices are overly long. Key algorithmic details are buried in pseudo-code; equation numbering and notation (e.g., inconsistent use of ceil/floor) is sometimes confusing.Significance: Adaptive downsampling addresses a genuine limitation of uniform pooling and benefits audio tasks. However, the high memory and runtime overhead, per-dataset tuning of σ, and unclear generalization to higher dimensions weaken practical impact. Limited discussion of deployment cost and sparse alternatives.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Presentation is generally coherent but overly verbose. The main contribution is novel, but numerous minor notation inconsistencies, lengthy appendices, and scattered hyperparameter details hinder clarity. Quality of experiments is good, covering realistic tasks, yet the complexity analysis and stability constraints require deeper theoretical or practical justification. Novelty is solid in the sequential downsampling domain, but the approach’s reliance on a critical hyperparameter σ and O(n²) matrix operations limits applicability. Reproducibility is aided by open-sourced code, but extensive appendix references and multiple training tricks (position loss, layer norm placement) must be carefully reproduced.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: AdaStride introduces a creative adaptive pooling mechanism with demonstrated audio gains. However, it incurs high computational cost, depends on sensitive hyperparameters, and has presentation and experimental gaps that undermine practical utility. While novel, the work is overshadowed by minor flaws and lack of broader justification, leading to a recommendation to reject.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper introduces AdaStride, a novel deep learning downsampling layer for sequential data that learns to vary stride sizes adaptively across a sequence, rather than using a fixed uniform ratio. Key to AdaStride is a vector positioning (VP) process that computes learned displacements for each time step, normalizes them onto a target output length, and builds a soft alignment matrix for weighted pooling. A fast variant, AdaStride-F, replaces dense matrix multiplication with scatter_add. The authors evaluate both versions on three tasks—audio classification, automatic speech recognition (ASR), and discrete representation learning with VQ-VAE—showing consistent gains over uniform pooling, strided convolution, spectral pooling, and DiffStride baselines. They analyze learned alignments, study the trade-off between aliasing and information preservation via the sharpness parameter σ, and report memory/time overhead and a lightweight AdaStride-S variant.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Originality: AdaStride’s per-step adaptive stride is a fresh idea extending both spatially varying pooling and learnable pooling approaches. The VP mechanism is a clever adaptation of alignment techniques to downsampling. Related work is thoroughly cited, though a deeper comparison to recent attention-based pooling would help.
Quality: Experiments are comprehensive across four audio classification datasets, LibriSpeech ASR with a Squeezeformer backbone, and VQ-VAE representation learning. Gains are consistent, and ablations (σ, position loss, hybrid LPF) are illuminating. However, theoretical insight into when and why VP succeeds over uniform or spectral methods is limited. The O(n²) cost of alignment and per-dataset σ tuning raise practical concerns.
Clarity: The paper is well organized with clear figures, pseudocode, and appendices covering reproducibility. Sections flow logically, and implementation details are provided. Some notation in the VP equations could be streamlined.
Significance: Adaptive downsampling addresses a real limitation of uniform pooling and benefits high-resolution sequence tasks. The consistent improvements suggest AdaStride will be of interest to audio and sequence-modeling practitioners. Yet, the quadratic complexity and hyperparameter sensitivity may limit broader adoption without further optimization.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The manuscript is clearly written and well structured, with full pseudocode, training details, and open-sourced code for reproduction. The contributions are methodologically sound, combining known alignment ideas with downsampling to yield a novel and effective technique. Novelty lies in adaptive stride deployment via VP; however, theoretical grounding is minimal. Reproducibility is high thanks to extensive appendices and released code. Overall, the paper balances clarity, technical quality, and practical validation effectively.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: AdaStride presents an innovative adaptive­stride downsampling layer for sequential DL tasks, backed by thorough empirical evaluation on audio classification, ASR, and VQ-VAE. The method is well motivated, clearly described, and reproducible. Key strengths include the VP alignment mechanism and comprehensive ablations. Limitations involve O(n²) computation, sensitivity to the σ hyperparameter, and limited theoretical analysis. Despite these, the paper offers a substantial empirical advance in sequence downsampling and merits acceptance.
